content: Blockchain is a way to record transactions securely without relying on a central bank or authority. Imagine a digital ledger that is shared across many independent participants—each keeps a copy and updates it together. When a transaction happens, it is grouped with others into a “block” and added to the chain of previous transactions. Instead of trusting one central institution, the network uses rules and checks to make sure everyone agrees on the transaction’s validity. This consensus prevents fraud and errors, making the record trustworthy and transparent. Because the ledger is distributed and constantly verified by many participants, it’s nearly impossible to alter past records without detection. For businesses, this means transactions can be processed securely and efficiently without intermediaries, reducing costs and delays. In short, blockchain replaces a central bank’s role with a secure, shared digital system that builds trust through collective agreement.
